austin.notes: Tech-driven demand slowdown has elevated sublease availability; large multifamily pipeline is moderating rent growth across the metro.

dallas.notes: Corporate relocations support absorption but a large suburban pipeline keeps overall vacancy elevated.

phoenix.notes: Diversifying economy and steady in-migration support demand; industrial and multifamily outperform office.

miami.notes: Financial services and tech migration has driven Class A office demand with limited new supply coming online.

atlanta.notes: Sublease pressure weighs on Class B/C assets; Midtown remains the most active submarket for new leasing activity.

denver.notes: Energy sector volatility and sustained remote work adoption have weakened office fundamentals across the metro.

charlotte.notes: Banking and fintech sector anchors demand; Uptown and South End continue to outperform suburban submarkets.

nashville.notes: Healthcare and entertainment tenants provide stable base demand; large apartment pipeline is moderating multifamily rent growth.

chicago.notes: Downtown office faces elevated sublease supply and limited new leasing; suburban demand remains mixed.

nyc.notes: Trophy and Class A Midtown assets recovering; outer boroughs and Class B product face continued headwinds.

los angeles.notes: Entertainment and tech pullback has significantly expanded sublease availability across West LA and Century City.

seattle.notes: Tech sector downsizing has produced significant sublease overhang; South Lake Union is the most severely affected submarket.

boston.notes: Life sciences demand supports lab/office hybrid product; traditional suburban office faces elevated vacancy.

washington.notes: Federal agency consolidations and lease contractions are increasing available space in core downtown submarkets.

houston.notes: Energy sector cyclicality and sustained corporate downsizing keep sublease pressure elevated across the metro.
